text: The surgeon attempts to locate the appendix, which is positioned retrocecal, but it is not visually identifiable at this stage of the procedure.
The surgeon mobilizes the cecum to rotate it, aiming to improve visibility of the appendix, which is in a retrocecal position.

The surgeon carefully mobilizes the cecum while being cautious of the right ureter, which is visible in the surgical field. The ureter's peristalsis is observed, confirming its location relative to the cecum.
The laparoscopic view shows the surgical field with magnification, revealing the area of dissection just a couple of centimeters away from the visible structures.
The surgeon applies traction to the peritoneum, and upon incision, the insufflated carbon dioxide expands the tissue planes, revealing the precise area for dissection.

The surgeon uses a monopolar cautery to perform sharp dissection of the appendix, which is in a retrocecal position. The ureter is identified and its course is noted as the cautery is applied in short bursts to carefully dissect the tissue.

The surgeon uses short bursts of monopolar cautery, causing carbon dioxide to expand and reveal a clear avascular plane for dissection. With traction and counter-traction, the appendix gradually becomes visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses cautery to address the lateral attachment of the peritoneum in the retroperitoneal area, revealing the exact location of the appendix, which is positioned in a retrosacral or sub-hepatic region.
The surgeon incises the fold near the appendix, revealing its proximity to the liver. The dissection progresses to a subhepatic level, with the appendix tip stopping just short of the liver.

The surgeon uses cautery to identify and dissect the plane around the appendix, dividing unconventional fascial attachments to visualize the appendix fully. The tubular structure of the appendix becomes more apparent, aiding in further dissection.

The surgeon applies traction and counter-traction to open the plane, revealing small thin fascial attachments. A short burst of monopolar cautery is used to manage minor bleeding, allowing for clear visualization of the surgical area.

The surgeon identifies small attachments tethering the appendix near its base, preparing to incise these to facilitate further dissection. The terminal ileum is visible, joining the cecum near the ileocecal junction.
The surgeon performs further dissection to clearly separate the cecum and appendix from the retroperitoneal structures, allowing for better visualization and easier manipulation.
The surgeon identifies the cecum and observes the path of the appendix. Small fascial attachments covering the appendix are incised to expose the appendix and mesoappendix.

The surgeon exposes the appendix and mesoappendix by incising peritoneal attachments, ensuring clear identification of the cecum to avoid mistaking similar-looking structures. The cecum is noted to be in close proximity during this process.

The surgeon uses the cecum as a landmark to guide the dissection, gradually removing small tissue attachments with monopolar cautery. The dissection progresses further as tissues are carefully taken down bit by bit.

The surgeon coagulates thin fascial attachments over the anti-mesenteric border of the appendix, releasing them to expose the bare appendix. The clear visualization allows for better identification and appreciation of the anatomical structures.

The surgeon identifies and holds thin fascial attachments around the appendix, carefully dissecting between them to create a clear window, leaving only the mesoappendix and appendix visible without further adhesions.

The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to dissect around the appendix, clearing the camera to improve visibility. With enhanced vision, the surgeon employs a combination of blunt and sharp dissection to identify the precise direction for further dissection.

The surgeon uses monopolar energy to sharply dissect the remaining facial attachments between the appendix and surrounding tissues. Small vessels and fat are visible within these attachments, requiring careful cautery application to separate them.

The surgeon continues the dissection, releasing attachments around the appendix. The tip of the appendix becomes visible in the surgical field, and the surgeon grasps it distally, applying traction to enhance visibility of the structure.

The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery combined with blunt dissection to meticulously open up tissue planes, revealing the area of the cecum. Care is taken to avoid injury to the cecum while further dissection removes attachments and adhesions, leaving the appendix exposed and bare.

The surgeon uses cautery to dissect attachments around the appendix, revealing a small gap between the appendix and the cecum. The tip of the appendix is visible, and the surgeon ensures a safe margin to prevent thermal injury to the cecum while continuing dissection.

The surgeon observes the translucency of the opposite side of the appendix, indicating the absence of major structures. This allows for easy coagulation without concern for injury or significant bleeding.
The surgeon continues the dissection in small increments, applying transverse traction to reveal thin attachments. The mesoappendix becomes visible in the plane, with vessels running behind it.

The surgeon dissects the area to identify small vessels and branches within the field. A fibrotic strand is divided, revealing a small vessel below. Above, smaller branches are visible, and the vessels are coagulated using a bipolar device and ligature for efficiency.

The surgeon twists the appendix to better visualize the attachments, pulling the mesoappendix away to isolate the appendix. This isolation reduces the size of the appendix, facilitating its removal through a tenon port with an endo loop applied distally.

The surgeon identifies the cecum and meticulously dissects at the base of the appendix, ensuring careful handling near the terminal small bowel to avoid thermal damage. The appendix is flipped to reveal additional adhesions, which are removed using an energy source. Small vessels from the appendicular branch of the ileocolic artery are visible and managed.

The surgeon carefully dissects and cauterizes small thin bands near the base of the appendix using energy sources, avoiding excessive use of monopolar cautery. The anterior and posterior leaves of the peritoneum are dissected separately for better control over the tissues. The camera is flipped laterally to inspect surrounding structures, ensuring they are away from the cecum.

The surgeon creates a clear window using cautery for adhesiolysis around the appendix, freeing it for the application of an endo loop. The appendix and the coning of the cecum are visible, with the surgeon carefully pulling tissues away from the cecum wall before applying energy. The dissection reaches the base, revealing supple tissue and triangulation near the cecum.

The surgeon identifies the tinea on the appendix, noting its location on the lateral, middle, and medial aspects. The base of the appendix is milked to push any fecolith into the distal portion. Instruments are used to hold the appendix, ensuring a firm area for applying the endo loop. The cecum is visible below the endo loop, appearing as if there is more appendix tissue, but it is actually the cecum.

The surgeon palpates the firm base of the appendix, applying two endo loops proximally with a 5 mm gap between them, followed by a third endo loop distally to prevent fecolith spillage. The appendix is chemically cauterized with butadiene-soaked gauze. A bubble walk is performed to examine the small bowel for Meckel's diverticulum. The specimen is removed through a ten mm port using claw forceps, ensuring the appendix does not contact the skin.
